response:
```json
{
  "diagnosis": "This job exhibits severe access granularity problems with 400,000 I/O operations performing only 3KB average transfer sizes, resulting in excessive syscall overhead. With 600MB total data and 200,000 writes/reads each, the job is making far too many small I/O calls instead of fewer large ones. The 399,992 unaligned accesses compound this problem by forcing additional overhead per operation.",
  
  "recommendations": [
    {
      "priority": 1,
      "bottleneck_dimension": "access_granularity", 
      "explanation": "The job performs 400,000 I/O operations with only 3KB average transfer size, causing massive syscall overhead. Each small write/read incurs kernel transition costs that dominate actual I/O time.",
      "code_before": "for (i=0; i<200000; i++) {\n    write(fd, buf, 3000);  /* 3KB per call */\n    read(fd, buf, 3000);   /* 3KB per call */\n}",
      "code_after": "/* Buffer small writes, flush in >=1MB chunks */\nchar big_buf[1048576];  /* 1MB buffer */\nsize_t offset = 0;\nfor (i=0; i<200000; i++) {\n    memcpy(big_buf + offset, buf, 3000);\n    offset += 3000;\n    if (offset >= 1048576) {\n        write(fd, big_buf, offset);\n        offset = 0;\n    }\n}\nif (offset > 0) write(fd, big_buf, offset);  /* flush remainder */",
      "expected_speedup": "16x based on reducing 400,000 syscalls to ~600 syscalls (600MB / 1MB chunks)",
      "kb_citation": "ior_ior_misaligned_t3000_n4_r2_116",
      "confidence": "high",
      "api_change": "POSIX write buffering strategy"
    }
  ],
  
  "overall_expected_improvement": "10-20x speedup by eliminating syscall overhead through write buffering"
}
```
system_prompt:
You are an HPC I/O performance expert. You analyze Darshan profiling data
and provide specific, actionable code-level optimization recommendations.

RULES:
1. Every recommendation MUST be grounded in the benchmark evidence provided.
2. Include specific before/after code snippets showing the exact change.
3. Cite the KB entry ID for each recommendation.
4. Quantify expected improvement using benchmark measurements only.
5. Do NOT fabricate performance numbers or API calls that don't exist.
6. Prioritize recommendations by expected impact (highest speedup first).
7. Use standard HPC I/O APIs: POSIX (read/write), MPI-IO (MPI_File_*), HDF5 (H5D*).
user_prompt:
Analyze this HPC job's I/O behavior and provide code-level optimization recommendations.

## Detected Bottlenecks (ML classifier, Micro-F1=0.923):
  - **access_granularity** (confidence=1.00): I/O operations with very small transfer sizes (<1MB), causing excessive syscall overhead

## Key Contributing Features (SHAP per-label attribution):

  access_granularity:
    - POSIX_FILE_NOT_ALIGNED = 399992.0000 (|SHAP|=3.7405, increases_risk)
    - POSIX_ACCESS1_COUNT = 400000.0000 (|SHAP|=1.8909, increases_risk)
    - POSIX_WRITES = 200000.0000 (|SHAP|=1.4026, increases_risk)
    - has_apmpi = 0.0000 (|SHAP|=1.0818, decreases_risk)
    - POSIX_READS = 200000.0000 (|SHAP|=0.8336, increases_risk)


## Benchmark Evidence (verified Knowledge Base with source code):

  --- KB Entry 1 (ID: ior_ior_misaligned_t3000_n4_r2_116) ---
  Benchmark: ior | Scenario: ior_misaligned_t3000_n4_r2
  Bottleneck: access_granularity
  Source: https://github.com/hpc/ior (C)
  Code (POSIX): write(fd, buf, transferSize)
  Code (MPIIO_indep): MPI_File_write(fh, buf, transferSize, MPI_BYTE, &status)
  Cause: I/O operations with very small transfer sizes (<1MB), causing excessive system call overhead
  Fix: Increase transfer/buffer size to >=1MB. For POSIX: aggregate small writes into large buffers. For MPI-IO: use collective I/O with larger datatypes.
  Code BEFORE:
    for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
  Code AFTER:
    /* Buffer small writes, flush in large chunks */
memcpy(big_buf + offset, buf, 64); offset += 64;
if (offset >= 1048576) { write(fd, big_buf, offset); offset = 0; }

  --- KB Entry 2 (ID: ior_ior_misaligned_t3000_n4_r1_115) ---
  Benchmark: ior | Scenario: ior_misaligned_t3000_n4_r1
  Bottleneck: access_granularity
  Source: https://github.com/hpc/ior (C)
  Code (POSIX): write(fd, buf, transferSize)
  Code (MPIIO_indep): MPI_File_write(fh, buf, transferSize, MPI_BYTE, &status)
  Cause: I/O operations with very small transfer sizes (<1MB), causing excessive system call overhead
  Fix: Increase transfer/buffer size to >=1MB. For POSIX: aggregate small writes into large buffers. For MPI-IO: use collective I/O with larger datatypes.
  Code BEFORE:
    for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
  Code AFTER:
    /* Buffer small writes, flush in large chunks */
memcpy(big_buf + offset, buf, 64); offset += 64;
if (offset >= 1048576) { write(fd, big_buf, offset); offset = 0; }

  --- KB Entry 3 (ID: ior_ior_misaligned_t3000_n4_r3_117) ---
  Benchmark: ior | Scenario: ior_misaligned_t3000_n4_r3
  Bottleneck: access_granularity
  Source: https://github.com/hpc/ior (C)
  Code (POSIX): write(fd, buf, transferSize)
  Code (MPIIO_indep): MPI_File_write(fh, buf, transferSize, MPI_BYTE, &status)
  Cause: I/O operations with very small transfer sizes (<1MB), causing excessive system call overhead
  Fix: Increase transfer/buffer size to >=1MB. For POSIX: aggregate small writes into large buffers. For MPI-IO: use collective I/O with larger datatypes.
  Code BEFORE:
    for (i=0; i<n; i++) write(fd, buf, 64);  /* 64B per call */
  Code AFTER:
    /* Buffer small writes, flush in large chunks */
memcpy(big_buf + offset, buf, 64); offset += 64;
if (offset >= 1048576) { write(fd, big_buf, offset); offset = 0; }


## Job Darshan Summary:
  nprocs: 4.0
  runtime_seconds: 1.8419
  POSIX_BYTES_WRITTEN: 600000000.0
  avg_write_size: 3000.0
